sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 61-year-old male, presents with a three-month history of a persistent cough, initially noticed while loading horses and exacerbating after a cold.
He denies any pain, shortness of breath, or other symptoms.
He has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly.
He is a former smoker, having stopped seven years ago.
He has a family history of breast cancer in his mother, but no history of lung cancer or other malignancies.
His current exercise regimen includes regular biking, which he enjoys.
Objective:
Vital signs: blood pressure 124/76, heart rate 70, respiration rate 16, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, afebrile.

Physical exam: lungs are clear and equal bilateral, heart has a regular rate and rhythm with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs, skin is pink, warm, and dry with no edema to the lower extremities, and there is mild swelling to the knuckles on both hands, which is consistent with rheumatoid arthritis.
Test Results: Chest CT scan shows a solitary 2 cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, which is smooth in appearance and not emphysematous.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule, which is likely to be a benign nodule but warrants further evaluation due to his smoking history.
Problem list: Rheumatoid arthritis; incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule, active; smoking history.
Plan:
I will order a pulmonary function test (PFT) to establish a baseline for the patient's lung function.
I will also schedule a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.

I plan to remove the nodule via video-assisted thoracoscopy, which involves making three small incisions on the side of the patient's right chest, and removing the nodule along with a small portion of the lung.
The patient will be under general anesthesia for approximately 1.5 hours, and will have a chest tube in place until the following day.
I anticipate the patient will be admitted for one night and discharged the next day.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient again in a few minutes to complete the necessary paperwork and begin the treatment process.
